observation: Linear's homepage hero shows a bold headline, minimal subtext, a product UI screenshot, and top-right nav CTAs but no hero-level CTA button driving conversion.
verdict: Strong visual brand but a critical missing hero CTA and competing distractions create unnecessary friction for an evaluator ready to act.

cta_clarity.note: No hero CTA button — 'Sign up' is only accessible via the top-right nav corner, easy to overlook for a first-time visitor.

copy_quality.note: Headline is punchy and modern, but 'teams and agents' may momentarily confuse buyers unfamiliar with AI-agent tooling.

flow_smoothness.note: Competing focal points (hero headline vs. 'Issue tracking is dead' announcement link) split attention before the visitor has oriented.

first_impression: Visually striking and premium-feeling, but the lack of a prominent hero CTA creates a dead end for motivated buyers ready to convert.
friction_points: No above-the-fold CTA button — an engineering manager ready to sign up must hunt for the small top-right 'Sign up' link rather than clicking an obvious primary action.

The 'Issue tracking is dead → linear.app/next' announcement competes with the main value proposition, potentially pulling evaluators off the conversion path before they've even understood the product.
Zero pricing signal or free-tier mention in the hero means a cost-conscious EM cannot immediately gauge fit without additional navigation.
recommendations: Add a high-contrast 'Start for free' CTA button directly in the hero section beneath the subtext to give buyers an immediate next step.
Move or visually de-emphasize the 'Issue tracking is dead' announcement banner so it doesn't compete with the primary conversion message.
Add a one-line trust anchor in the hero (e.g., 'Free plan available — no credit card required') to reduce pricing uncertainty before the buyer scrolls.
persona: Engineering manager at a mid-size SaaS company evaluating issue-tracking tools as an alternative to Jira or Shortcut

observation: Homepage hero is confirmed: large headline 'The product development system for teams and agents', minimal subtext, a product UI screenshot partially visible, and 'Sign up' is only in the top-right nav — no hero-level CTA button anywhere in the fold.
verdict: Visually excellent but structurally weak on conversion — no hero CTA and no pricing signal create real friction for a buyer persona with a clear intent to evaluate.

cta_clarity.note: The only conversion action ('Sign up') is a small nav button — no below-headline CTA to guide an evaluator who just read the value prop.

copy_quality.note: 'Purpose-built for planning and building products. Designed for the AI era.' is clean and differentiated, but 'agents' still needs a beat of mental parsing for an EM coming from Jira.

flow_smoothness.note: The 'Issue tracking is dead → linear.app/next' link sits visually opposite the subtext and competes for the evaluator's next click without being a product action.

first_impression: Premium, developer-focused aesthetic that signals quality, but the absence of a hero CTA and pricing hint leaves a motivated EM with no clear next step.
friction_points: No hero CTA button means an EM ready to start a trial must backtrack to a small nav link rather than clicking an obvious primary action at the point of peak interest.
The 'Issue tracking is dead' announcement link is an off-ramp to a manifesto page, not a product action — it could derail an evaluator mid-funnel before they've engaged with features or pricing.
No free-plan or pricing signal anywhere above the fold forces an EM comparing budget options to navigate away just to answer the basic 'can we afford this?' question.
recommendations: Add a prominent 'Start for free — no credit card required' button directly beneath the subtext in the hero to capture intent at peak engagement.
Relocate the 'Issue tracking is dead' announcement to a subdued banner above the nav or a blog/news section lower on the page so it doesn't compete with the conversion path.
Add a one-liner pricing anchor (e.g., 'Free for small teams · Scales with you') in the hero subtext area to pre-qualify buyers before they scroll or navigate away.

observation: Pricing page shows four tiers (Free $0, Basic $10, Business $16, Enterprise Custom) with yearly-billing toggles active by default and feature lists visible above the fold.
verdict: Pricing tiers are legible and well-structured, but missing CTAs and an opaque billing-toggle pattern create real friction for an EM trying to move from evaluation to action.

cta_clarity.note: No 'Get started' or 'Try free' CTA buttons are visible under any pricing tier — an EM ready to choose a plan has no clear action to take from this view.

copy_quality.note: Tier names and prices are clean, but 'All Free features +' and 'All Basic features +' are lazy shorthand — an EM comparing plans must mentally chain three tiers to understand what Business actually includes.

flow_smoothness.note: The yearly-billing toggle is pre-selected without showing the monthly alternative, which means an EM can't immediately see the cost delta and feels nudged rather than informed.

first_impression: Pricing structure is clear and competitive at a glance, but the absence of per-tier CTAs and a hidden monthly-price comparison creates a stall point for a buyer ready to commit.
friction_points: No CTA buttons under any plan tier mean an EM who has decided on Business has no direct path to start a trial or sign up from the pricing page itself.
The 'Billed yearly' toggle defaults to ON without displaying the monthly price, so an EM evaluating total cost of ownership for budget approval cannot see the month-to-month rate without toggling.

The '250 issues' cap on the Free plan is a critical limit for a mid-size engineering team, but there is no callout or tooltip explaining what counts as an issue — leaving the EM uncertain whether Free is even a viable trial option.
recommendations: Add a prominent 'Get started free' or 'Start trial' CTA button beneath each paid tier card so buyers have an immediate action at the point of decision.
Show both yearly and monthly prices simultaneously (e.g., '$10/user/mo billed yearly · $14 month-to-month') rather than hiding one behind a toggle.
Add a tooltip or inline note on the Free plan's '250 issues' limit clarifying what constitutes an issue and linking to a FAQ so EMs can assess trial viability without leaving the page.

observation: Pricing page screenshot confirmed: four tiers (Free $0, Basic $10, Business $16, Enterprise Custom) with 'Billed yearly' toggles active on Basic and Business, feature lists visible, but zero CTA buttons under any tier.
verdict: Pricing information is legible but the page fails its primary job — no CTA under any tier means motivated buyers must work to convert, a critical usability gap.

cta_clarity.note: There are zero CTA buttons on this pricing page — an EM who has chosen a plan has absolutely no action to take without navigating back to the top-right 'Sign up' link.

copy_quality.note: 'All Free features +' additive shorthand forces an EM to mentally chain three tiers to understand the full Business feature set, adding unnecessary cognitive load during evaluation.

flow_smoothness.note: The 'Billed yearly' toggle is on by default with no side-by-side monthly price displayed, so an EM justifying budget to finance cannot see the month-to-month alternative without manually toggling.

first_impression: Clean tier layout and competitive price points, but the complete absence of any CTA button on a pricing page is a serious conversion failure for an EM ready to act.
friction_points: No CTA buttons exist under any of the four pricing tiers, forcing an EM who has decided on a plan to hunt for the small top-right 'Sign up' nav link rather than converting at peak intent.

The '250 issues' cap on the Free plan has no qualifying tooltip — an EM managing a 15-person team cannot assess whether Free is a viable trial without knowing if archived/closed issues count toward the cap.
Enterprise shows 'Custom' with no 'Contact sales' button or ballpark range, leaving an EM evaluating for a larger org with no path forward from the pricing page itself.
recommendations: Add a clearly labeled CTA button ('Get started free', 'Start Basic trial', etc.) beneath each tier card so buyers can convert immediately at the point of decision.
Add a hover tooltip or inline footnote on '250 issues' clarifying the counting logic (e.g., active issues only) so EMs can confidently assess trial viability.

Replace the static 'Custom' Enterprise label with a visible 'Contact sales' button and a one-line anchor (e.g., 'Typically $XX+/user — volume discounts available') to give large-team evaluators a next step.

observation: Customers page shows a filterable logo grid with Coinbase, OpenAI, and Cursor as featured stories — strong brand-name social proof above the fold with industry filter tabs (SaaS, AI, Fintech, etc.).
verdict: Strong individual social proof stories but the page skews toward AI-native outliers and lacks aggregate trust signals and conversion hooks that a mid-market SaaS EM needs.

cta_clarity.note: 'Read story' and 'Watch video' are present but low-contrast and small — a motivated EM would notice them, but they don't compel action the way a 'See how they use Linear' CTA would.

copy_quality.note: Story headlines like 'Why OpenAI chose Linear and scaled to 3,000 users' are specific and outcome-focused, which resonates with an EM doing vendor comparison.

flow_smoothness.note: The SaaS filter tab is present but not pre-selected — an EM landing here sees AI-first companies (OpenAI, Cursor) as top stories, which may feel less directly relevant to their own use case.

first_impression: Impressive brand-name logos deliver instant credibility, but the featured stories skew heavily toward AI companies rather than typical mid-market SaaS engineering teams.
friction_points: The default 'Featured' view surfaces AI-native companies (OpenAI, Cursor, Coinbase) as top stories — an EM at a mid-size SaaS company must manually click the 'SaaS' filter to find peers they can benchmark against.

There is no quantified social proof on this page (e.g., '10,000 teams' or '500+ companies') — an EM looking for adoption scale to justify switching from Jira finds only individual stories with no aggregate numbers.

No CTA exists anywhere on this page to start a trial or learn about migration from Jira/Shortcut — an EM whose confidence is just peaked by a customer story has no conversion hook to act on immediately.
recommendations: Default the filter to 'SaaS' for visitors arriving via product-comparison channels, or add a 'Teams like yours' section that surfaces mid-market SaaS stories before AI-native outliers.
Add a page-level headline stat (e.g., 'Trusted by 10,000+ engineering teams') above the filter tabs to give evaluators a scale signal alongside the individual stories.
Add a sticky or end-of-story CTA (e.g., 'Ready to switch? Start free or talk to us about migrating from Jira') to capture intent while social proof is freshest.

observation: SaaS filter is now active, showing Clay, Clio, and Retool stories — the Clio headline 'How Linear helps engineering managers at Clio operate more efficiently' is directly on-persona for this evaluator.
verdict: The SaaS-filtered customer page is relevant and well-copy-matched for this persona, but weak CTAs and missing scale/context metadata reduce its conversion effectiveness.

cta_clarity.note: 'Read story →' is the only action on each card — it's understated, low-contrast gray text that doesn't compel clicks the way a button would for a buyer scanning quickly.

copy_quality.note: Story headlines are outcome-focused and role-specific (Clio card directly calls out 'engineering managers'), which is highly relevant for this persona's evaluation.

flow_smoothness.note: The SaaS filter tab cleanly narrows the grid to relevant peers — Clay, Clio, Retool are all recognizable mid-market SaaS companies an EM would benchmark against.

first_impression: Filtered SaaS view surfaces highly relevant peer companies and role-specific headlines that directly validate Linear for an engineering manager's use case.
friction_points: The 'Read story →' CTA is rendered in low-contrast gray text rather than a button, making it easy to overlook during a fast scan — especially on the dark background.
There are only 3 SaaS stories visible with no indication of how many exist in total — an EM might assume this is a thin reference base and underestimate Linear's SaaS adoption.
No team-size or scale metadata is shown on the cards (e.g., '200-person eng team') — an EM at a mid-size company can't quickly gauge whether these customer stories reflect their own org complexity.
recommendations: Replace the 'Read story →' text links with pill-shaped or outlined buttons to increase tap target size and visual affordance for CTAs on the dark background.
Add a story count label next to the SaaS filter tab (e.g., 'SaaS · 12') so evaluators know the breadth of references available before clicking.
Add a one-line metadata tag to each card (e.g., 'Series B · 300 employees') so an EM can self-qualify story relevance without clicking through to read the full case study.

observation: Clio customer story page opens with a bold, on-persona headline ('How Linear helps engineering managers at Clio operate more efficiently') and a large Clio logo hero image — no body content or metrics visible yet above the fold.
verdict: Linear's site has strong visual design and well-targeted copy, but systemic CTA absence — on the homepage, pricing page, customers page, and case study — creates repeated conversion failures that would frustrate a motivated engineering manager buyer across the entire evaluation journey.

cta_clarity.note: No CTA button exists above the fold on this case study page — an EM whose confidence has just peaked on the headline has zero conversion action available without scrolling.

copy_quality.note: The headline is perfectly role-targeted ('engineering managers') and outcome-specific ('operate more efficiently'), making it the strongest copy encountered across the entire evaluation.

flow_smoothness.note: The hero image is a large blue logo card with no supporting context — the EM must scroll before receiving any substance, adding a dead-zone beat to the reading flow.

first_impression: The headline is the most persuasive copy on the site for this persona, but the full-bleed logo hero with no accompanying summary stat or pull quote wastes the high-intent moment.
friction_points: No CTA or 'Start free' button appears above the fold at the highest-intent point in the journey — an EM who just read a perfectly matched headline has no immediate path to act.
The large logo hero image pushes all substantive content (metrics, quotes, outcomes) below the fold, forcing a scroll before the EM gets any evidence to justify the story's headline promise.
There is no breadcrumb-level context about Clio's team size or stack visible above the fold, so an EM cannot immediately confirm this story is peer-relevant before investing time reading.
recommendations: Add a sticky 'Get started free' or 'Try Linear' CTA button that appears in the page hero or as a fixed sidebar element so the EM can act at peak conviction.
Replace or shrink the full-bleed logo hero to make room for a 2–3 sentence summary and a key metric (e.g., '40% faster sprint cycles') visible without scrolling.
Add a one-line company metadata tag beneath the headline (e.g., 'Clio · 800 employees · Legal SaaS · Series E') so an EM can instantly gauge story relevance before reading.

observation: Linear pricing page shows 4 tiers (Free, Basic $10, Business $16, Enterprise Custom) with yearly billing toggled on by default and no CTA buttons visible above the fold.
verdict: Strong visual clarity on mid-tier plans, but hidden CTAs and opaque Enterprise pricing create meaningful friction for a buyer ready to make a decision.

cta_clarity.note: No 'Get started' or 'Contact sales' buttons are visible above the fold, leaving the buyer without a clear next step.

copy_quality.note: Tier names and feature lists are concise and scannable, though 'Agent platform' and 'Triage Intelligence' need a tooltip for unfamiliar buyers.

flow_smoothness.note: Yearly billing is defaulted on with no monthly toggle visible above fold, forcing extra interaction to compare true monthly cost.

first_impression: Clean, minimal layout with visible prices, but critical CTAs are hidden and the yearly-default billing obscures the monthly cost comparison this persona needs.
friction_points: Yearly billing is toggled on by default, so the engineering manager can't immediately see monthly pricing without extra interaction — slowing budget comparison against competitors.
No CTA buttons are visible above the fold; the buyer has no obvious way to start a trial or contact sales without scrolling.
Enterprise plan shows only 'Custom' with zero pricing ballpark, making it impossible for this persona to determine whether Enterprise is even in their budget range.
recommendations: Show both monthly and yearly prices simultaneously (e.g., 'Save 20% yearly') or default to monthly to reduce cognitive friction during initial evaluation.
Surface 'Get started free' and 'Contact sales' CTAs within each pricing card at or near the fold so the next action is always visible.
Add an estimated Enterprise price range or employee-count threshold (e.g., '50+ seats, typically $XX–$XX/user') to let buyers self-qualify before calling sales.
persona: Engineering manager at a 20–50 person startup evaluating issue tracking tools for their team

observation: Pricing page shows 4 tiers with 'Billed yearly' toggles active per card; no global billing toggle is visible, and no CTA buttons appear above the fold.
verdict: Pricing data is clear but actionability is weak — missing above-fold CTAs and fragmented billing toggles slow down a buyer who is ready to decide.

cta_clarity.note: No 'Get started' or 'Contact sales' buttons are visible anywhere above the fold — the buyer has no actionable next step after reviewing prices.

copy_quality.note: Tier names and feature lists are clean and scannable, but 'Triage Intelligence' and 'Agent platform' are jargon-heavy terms with no inline explanation.

flow_smoothness.note: Per-card billing toggles are an unusual pattern — a single global toggle would reduce interaction cost for a buyer comparing all plans at once.

first_impression: Prices are immediately visible and layout is clean, but the absence of CTAs above the fold and per-card billing toggles add unnecessary friction for a buyer ready to act.
friction_points: The 'Billed yearly' toggle lives inside each pricing card rather than as a single global control, so an engineering manager comparing monthly vs. yearly costs must click multiple times.
No CTA buttons (e.g., 'Start free trial', 'Get started', 'Contact sales') are visible above the fold, leaving the buyer with no clear conversion path after reviewing prices.
Enterprise is labeled 'Annual billing only' with 'Custom' pricing — a startup EM can't self-qualify or estimate cost without initiating a sales conversation.
recommendations: Replace per-card billing toggles with a single global 'Monthly / Yearly' toggle at the top of the pricing section to let buyers compare all plans in one click.
Add a prominent CTA button beneath each plan's price (above the fold) so buyers can immediately act on whichever tier fits their needs.
Add a minimum seat count or indicative price range to the Enterprise card (e.g., 'From $XX/user for 50+ seats') so buyers can self-qualify before contacting sales.

observation: Full above-the-fold pricing page is now visible: 4 plan cards (Free $0, Basic $10, Business $16, Enterprise Custom) with per-card 'Billed yearly' toggles active on Basic and Business, but zero CTA buttons visible anywhere on screen.
verdict: Pricing information is readable but completely non-actionable above the fold — the total absence of CTAs is a critical blocker for any buyer ready to convert.

cta_clarity.note: There are zero CTA buttons visible on the entire visible pricing page — no 'Get started', 'Try free', or 'Contact sales' — leaving the buyer with no conversion path.

copy_quality.note: Plan names, prices, and feature lists are concise and well-structured, but 'Triage Intelligence' and 'Agent platform' are undefined terms that would confuse a first-time evaluator.

flow_smoothness.note: Per-card billing toggles (Basic and Business each have their own) create extra clicks and inconsistency — Free and Enterprise have no toggle at all, breaking the pattern.

first_impression: Pricing is clearly laid out with visible dollar amounts, but the complete absence of CTAs is a critical failure — an engineering manager ready to sign up has nowhere to go.
friction_points: No CTA buttons exist anywhere on the visible page — a buyer who has decided on a plan cannot start a trial, sign up, or contact sales without scrolling to find a hidden button or leaving the page.

Per-card 'Billed yearly' toggles on only Basic and Business (Free and Enterprise have none) is an inconsistent pattern that forces the EM to mentally reconcile why only two plans have toggleable billing.
The Free plan lists '250 issues' as a hard cap with no mention of what happens when you hit it — a 20-person team would likely exceed this quickly and needs to understand the upgrade trigger.
recommendations: Add a clearly labeled CTA button beneath each plan's price (e.g., 'Start free', 'Get Basic', 'Try Business', 'Contact sales') so buyers can act immediately after choosing a plan.
Replace the two per-card toggles with a single global 'Monthly / Yearly (save X%)' toggle at the top of the pricing section for a consistent, lower-effort comparison experience.
Add a tooltip or footnote on the Free plan explaining what happens at the 250-issue limit (e.g., 'Need more? Upgrade anytime to Basic for unlimited issues') to reduce upgrade anxiety.

observation: Screenshot confirms 4 plan cards (Free $0, Basic $10/mo, Business $16/mo, Enterprise Custom) with per-card 'Billed yearly' toggles on Basic and Business, plus a 'Sign up' CTA in the global nav — but no per-plan CTAs beneath each card's price.
verdict: Pricing data is readable but the purchase path is unclear — missing per-plan CTAs and inconsistent billing toggles create unnecessary friction for a buyer ready to decide.

cta_clarity.note: Only a global 'Sign up' nav CTA exists — there are no per-plan action buttons beneath each tier, so a buyer can't directly select the Business or Enterprise plan they've chosen.

copy_quality.note: Prices, tier names, and feature lists are clear and scannable, but 'Triage Intelligence' and 'Agent platform (beta)' lack tooltips and will confuse first-time evaluators.

flow_smoothness.note: Per-card billing toggles on only two of four plans creates an inconsistent pattern, and the lack of a global toggle forces multiple interactions to compare monthly vs. yearly costs.

first_impression: Clean, minimal pricing layout with visible prices, but the absence of per-plan CTAs and inconsistent billing toggles slow down a buyer ready to act.
friction_points: No per-plan CTA buttons (e.g., 'Get Basic', 'Start Business trial') sit beneath each card — an EM who picks Business has to use the generic nav 'Sign up' with no plan pre-selected.
Basic ($10) and Business ($16) show 'Billed yearly' toggles but Free and Enterprise have no toggle, making it unclear whether the displayed prices are monthly or already-discounted annual rates.
The Free plan's '250 issues' cap is listed without any upgrade prompt or guidance, leaving a team evaluating feasibility unable to quickly gauge whether Free is viable before committing.
recommendations: Add a dedicated CTA button under each plan price (e.g., 'Start for free', 'Get Basic', 'Try Business', 'Contact sales') to let buyers act on their chosen tier directly.
Replace the two per-card billing toggles with a single global 'Monthly / Yearly (save 20%)' toggle above all cards so the billing cadence is immediately transparent across all plans.
Add a short note under the Free plan's issue cap (e.g., 'Hit the limit? Upgrade in one click — no data lost') to remove uncertainty about the upgrade path for growing teams.

observation: Full above-the-fold view confirmed: 4 plan cards visible, 'Billed yearly' toggles on Basic and Business cards only, 'Sign up' in nav, but zero per-plan CTA buttons beneath any pricing card.
verdict: Pricing is readable but not actionable — missing per-plan CTAs, ambiguous toggle labeling, and opaque Enterprise pricing create real friction for a buyer ready to commit.

cta_clarity.note: Only a single generic 'Sign up' nav button exists — no per-plan CTAs let the EM directly select the Business or Enterprise tier they've evaluated.

copy_quality.note: Prices and feature lists are clean and scannable, but 'Triage Intelligence' and 'Agent platform (beta)' have no tooltips or explanations for buyers unfamiliar with Linear's terminology.

flow_smoothness.note: Billing toggles appear only on Basic and Business — Free shows '$0' with no toggle and Enterprise says 'Annual billing only', creating an inconsistent and confusing billing-cadence experience across the four cards.

first_impression: Pricing is immediately legible with visible dollar amounts, but the lack of per-plan CTAs and inconsistent toggle placement undermine an otherwise clean layout.
friction_points: No per-plan CTA buttons exist beneath any plan card — an EM who has decided on Business must use the generic nav 'Sign up' with no plan context carried forward.

Basic shows '$10 per user/month' with a 'Billed yearly' toggle active, but it's unclear whether $10 is the already-discounted annual rate or the monthly rate — the toggle state doesn't clarify what the price reflects.
Enterprise's 'Custom' + 'Annual billing only' label gives a startup EM zero pricing signal, making it impossible to budget or self-qualify without initiating a sales call.
recommendations: Add a dedicated CTA button under each plan (e.g., 'Start free', 'Get Basic', 'Get Business', 'Contact sales') so buyers can act on their chosen tier directly.
Clarify the toggle label to show both prices — e.g., 'Billed yearly ($10/mo) | Monthly ($13/mo)' — so the displayed price is always unambiguous.
Add a pricing floor or seat-count threshold to the Enterprise card (e.g., 'Typically for teams of 50+, starting at $XX/user') so buyers can self-qualify without a sales call.

observation: Page has scrolled directly to the footer with a large empty dark area above it — no feature comparison table, FAQ section, or social proof is visible between the pricing cards and the footer.
verdict: The pricing page shows prices clearly but lacks the supporting content — comparison table, social proof, bottom CTAs — that a startup EM needs to confidently commit.

cta_clarity.note: The footer contains no CTAs — not even a 'Sign up' or 'Get started' link — meaning a buyer who scrolled through the whole page reaches a dead end with no conversion path.

copy_quality.note: Footer links are well-organized and reveal breadth of product (Agents, Insights, Diffs), but none of this context appears on the pricing page itself to reinforce the value of paid tiers.

flow_smoothness.note: There is no feature comparison table, FAQ, or social proof between the plan cards and the footer — an EM who scrolls past the cards hits a dead end with no supporting content to justify the purchase.

first_impression: The footer is comprehensive but the complete absence of mid-page supporting content — no comparison table, no testimonials, no FAQ — is a major gap for a buyer doing due diligence.
friction_points: No feature comparison table exists on the pricing page — an EM comparing Business vs. Basic must read each card in isolation with no side-by-side diff of what they're paying for.
Zero social proof (customer logos, team-size references, testimonials) appears anywhere on the pricing page, leaving a startup EM with no peer validation before committing.
No CTA appears in the footer or at the bottom of the page, so a buyer who scrolled the entire page has no obvious next action and must scroll back up to find the nav 'Sign up' button.
recommendations: Add a collapsible full feature comparison table below the plan cards so buyers can see a precise diff between Basic and Business without reading cards in isolation.
Add 3–5 customer logos or a short testimonial block (e.g., 'Used by teams at Vercel, Raycast, and Ramp') between the plan cards and footer to provide social validation at the decision moment.
Add a sticky bottom CTA bar or a repeated 'Get started' / 'Contact sales' button block at the bottom of the pricing page so buyers always have a clear next step.

observation: Full pricing page confirmed: 4 plan cards (Free $0, Basic $10, Business $16, Enterprise Custom) with 'Billed yearly' toggles active on Basic and Business only, 'Sign up' in nav, zero per-plan CTA buttons beneath any card.
verdict: Pricing data is clear and well-structured, but missing per-plan CTAs, ambiguous billing toggle labeling, and zero social proof collectively weaken the page's ability to convert a ready buyer.

cta_clarity.note: The only visible CTA is a generic 'Sign up' in the top nav — no per-plan buttons let the EM select Business or Enterprise directly from the pricing card.

copy_quality.note: Feature lists per tier are concise and additive ('All Basic features +'), but 'Triage Intelligence' and 'Agent platform (beta)' are undefined terms that require prior Linear knowledge to evaluate.

flow_smoothness.note: Billing toggles exist only on Basic and Business — Free shows flat '$0' and Enterprise shows 'Annual billing only' with no toggle — creating an inconsistent, confusing billing-cadence pattern across all four cards.

first_impression: Prices are clearly visible and the card layout is clean, but the absence of per-plan CTAs and inconsistent billing toggle placement leave a ready-to-buy EM without a clear next step.
friction_points: No per-plan CTA buttons exist beneath any pricing card — an EM who chooses Business must navigate away from the comparison view to start a trial, losing plan-selection context.
Basic shows '$10 per user/month' with a 'Billed yearly' toggle on, but the page never states the monthly equivalent (presumably ~$13), so the EM can't quickly calculate the annual budget commitment.
Zero social proof (logos, team-size references, or testimonials) appears anywhere on the pricing page, leaving a startup EM with no peer validation at the moment of purchase decision.
recommendations: Add a dedicated CTA button under each plan's price (e.g., 'Start free', 'Get Basic', 'Try Business', 'Contact sales') so the EM can act directly from the card they chose.
Show both billing rates on the toggle (e.g., '$10/mo billed yearly · $13/mo billed monthly') so the EM can instantly calculate annual spend without guesswork.
Add a strip of 4–6 recognizable customer logos or a short '10,000+ teams use Linear' headline directly below the plan cards to provide social validation at the decision moment.

observation: Navigation off the pricing page revealed a rich Customers page (Coinbase, OpenAI, Cursor case studies) — strong social proof that is entirely absent from the pricing page where it would most influence a buying decision.
verdict: Linear's pricing page clearly communicates prices but fails to convert — missing per-plan CTAs, no comparison table, and zero social proof on the page mean a startup EM leaves without enough context or a clear path to act.

cta_clarity.note: The only CTA on the pricing page is a generic 'Sign up' in the top nav — there are no per-plan action buttons on any of the four pricing cards, so a buyer who has chosen Business has no direct path to start that plan.

copy_quality.note: Tier names, prices, and additive feature lists ('All Basic features +') are clean and scannable, but 'Triage Intelligence' and 'Agent platform (beta)' are undefined terms that assume prior Linear knowledge.

flow_smoothness.note: The pricing page is essentially just 4 cards followed by a footer — no comparison table, no FAQ, no social proof, and no bottom CTA — leaving a buyer who scrolls the whole page at a dead end with no supporting content to justify the decision.

first_impression: Pricing is immediately legible with clear dollar amounts and a minimal layout, but the page lacks the supporting infrastructure — per-plan CTAs, comparison table, and social proof — that a startup EM needs to confidently commit.
friction_points: No per-plan CTA buttons exist on any pricing card — an EM who has decided on Business must use the generic nav 'Sign up' with no plan context pre-selected, adding unnecessary steps to conversion.

observation: The Linear Customers page displays a dark-themed grid of case study cards (Coinbase, OpenAI, Cursor visible) with category filters and minimal framing copy.
verdict: The page relies entirely on brand recognition of featured logos but fails to proactively communicate outcomes, leaving a skeptical engineering leader to do extra work to find ROI evidence.

cta_clarity.note: Low-contrast 'Read story →' / 'Watch video →' links are understated and inconsistent — a buyer can't predict content format at a glance.

copy_quality.note: The page title is simply 'Customers' with no supporting headline, outcome summary, or 'why it matters' framing to hook an evaluating buyer.

flow_smoothness.note: Clean grid layout with visible category filters makes navigation intuitive, but no scroll cue signals more content below.

first_impression: Visually polished but editorially thin — prestigious logos are present but zero outcome data is visible above the fold to compel a buyer to keep reading.
friction_points: No page-level value proposition or aggregate social proof (e.g., '500+ teams ship faster with Linear') means the buyer gets no persuasive hook before seeing individual stories.
Card thumbnails show no quantitative results — the buyer must click into each story to determine relevance, adding unnecessary discovery effort.
Inconsistent CTA labels ('Read story' vs. 'Watch video') without format icons create micro-uncertainty about what clicking will deliver.
recommendations: Add a one-line hero subheading with a concrete aggregate metric (e.g., 'Trusted by 500+ product teams — see how they ship faster') directly under 'Customers'.
Surface one key result metric on each card (e.g., '3,000 users onboarded') so buyers can triage relevance without clicking through.
Standardise CTA labels with a format badge or icon (📄 Story / 🎬 Video) so content type is scannable at a glance.
persona: VP of Engineering at a growth-stage B2B SaaS startup evaluating Linear against Jira or Linear competitors for their 20–80 person dev team

observation: Full above-the-fold view of Linear's Customers page confirms a dark-themed grid with Coinbase, OpenAI, and Cursor cards visible, plus partial thumbnails of a second row — category filters (Featured, SaaS, AI, Fintech, Consumer, Hardware, Health, Enterprise) are present.
verdict: The page has strong brand credibility via logos but underdelivers on persuasive evidence, leaving a skeptical VP to do manual effort to determine which stories are worth reading.

first_impression: Prestigious logos create immediate credibility, but the VP will notice most cards lack outcome data and feel more like a logo wall than a proof-of-value gallery.
friction_points: As a buyer comparing tools, I can't quickly assess ROI relevance from card titles alone — only OpenAI's card hints at a measurable outcome ('3,000 users'), forcing me to click into each story to evaluate fit.
The 'Featured' filter tab has no active/selected visual state, so I'm uncertain whether I'm seeing a curated subset or the full customer list — this undermines trust in the page's organization.
There is no page-level anchor explaining what types of teams or use cases are represented, so I don't know if the stories are relevant to a 50-person SaaS engineering team before clicking.
recommendations: Add a one-line outcome metric (e.g., '↑40% faster cycles' or '3,000 users onboarded') as a subline on every card so buyers can triage relevance without clicking through.
Apply a clear active-state style (underline, highlight, or pill background) to the currently selected filter tab so users know which view they're in.

Add a two-sentence page intro below 'Customers' naming the breadth of use cases represented (e.g., 'From 10-person startups to 3,000-seat enterprises — see how software teams ship faster with Linear').

observation: The Customers page is fully visible with three featured cards: Coinbase ('bet on agent-first development'), OpenAI ('scaled to 3,000 users'), and Cursor ('Watch video'), plus a second row partially visible — category filters are at the top.
verdict: The page has strong logo credibility but inconsistent and outcome-weak card copy will slow down a VP trying to efficiently assess Linear's fit for their team.

cta_clarity.note: 'Read story →' and 'Watch video →' are functional but rendered as small gray ghost text with no button affordance, making them easy to miss on a fast scan.

copy_quality.note: Coinbase's card title ('bet on agent-first development') describes a strategic posture, not an outcome — a VP evaluating tools wants to know what Linear specifically enabled, not Coinbase's internal philosophy.

flow_smoothness.note: The grid layout and filter tabs are clean and scannable, but the second row of cards is cut off with no visual scroll cue, leaving unclear whether more content exists.

friction_points: As a VP evaluating tools, Coinbase's headline 'bet on agent-first development' tells me nothing about what Linear did for their team — it reads as Coinbase's internal strategy, not a Linear outcome.
The 'Watch video' CTA on Cursor breaks the content-type consistency of the grid — I have to read every card's CTA individually to know what I'm clicking into, adding cognitive overhead.
The second row of cards is partially cropped with no scroll indicator, so I can't tell at a glance how many customer stories exist or whether a story from my industry (SaaS) is featured.
recommendations: Rewrite card titles to lead with the Linear-specific outcome (e.g., 'How Coinbase accelerated agent-first delivery with Linear') so buyers immediately understand the tool's role.
Add a small format badge (e.g., '📄 Case study' or '🎬 Video') to each card thumbnail so content type is scannable without reading the CTA text.

observation: Successfully landed on the OpenAI case study page with a strong headline ('Why OpenAI chose Linear and scaled to 3,000 users') and a full-width branded hero image — story content is below the fold.
verdict: Strong outcome-led headline but the oversized hero and absent CTA squander the conversion opportunity at the exact moment buyer intent is highest.

copy_quality.note: The headline is concrete and outcome-led ('scaled to 3,000 users') — exactly the kind of signal a VP wants — but all supporting detail is hidden below the fold.

first_impression: The headline lands well and immediately validates the click, but the large decorative hero image delays access to the actual case study content a VP came here to read.
friction_points: The hero image occupies nearly the full viewport with no story teaser beneath it, forcing the VP to scroll blind without knowing what depth or format of content awaits them.
There is no CTA (e.g., 'Start free trial' or 'Talk to sales') anchored above the fold where buyer intent is highest — immediately after seeing a compelling headline is the best conversion moment.
No metadata (read time, author, date, or company size context) is visible, so a VP can't quickly judge whether OpenAI's 3,000-user scale story is relevant to their 20–80 person team.
recommendations: Reduce the hero image height to half-viewport and show the first paragraph or a pull-quote below it so buyers immediately see content value on load.
Add a persistent sticky CTA bar ('Start free trial' or 'Book a demo') that appears as the user scrolls through the case study, anchored to the persuasion moment.
Add a small metadata strip under the headline (e.g., 'Industry: AI · Team size: 3,000+ · Use case: Engineering') so buyers can immediately assess story relevance to their context.

verdict: The hero nails tone and visual confidence but fails the evaluating buyer on every practical UX dimension — no wayfinding, no time signal, and no conversion path.
